back_translation: But this matter was an exceedingly great calamity to Jonah, and he became intensely angry.

He prayed to the LORD, saying: "O LORD, was this not what I said while I was still in my homeland? This is why I rushed to flee to Tarshish, because I knew that you are a gracious and compassionate God, slow to anger, abounding in covenant-mercy, and one who relents from punishment.
Now, O LORD, please take my life from me, for it is better for me to die than to live."
The LORD said, "Is it right for you to be this angry?"
Jonah went out from the city and sat down east of the city; he made himself a shelter there and sat under its shade, waiting to see what would happen to the city.
The LORD God appointed a gourd plant, and it grew up over Jonah to provide shade over his head, to deliver him from his distress; and Jonah was greatly delighted because of the plant.
But God appointed a worm at the dawn of the next day, which gnawed at the gourd so that it withered.
When the sun rose, God appointed a scorching east wind; the sun beat down on Jonah's head, and he grew faint, asking that he might die, saying, "It is better for me to die than to live."
God said to Jonah, "Is it right for you to be angry about the gourd plant?" Jonah replied, "It is exceedingly right for me to be angry — angry enough to die."
The LORD said, "You cared about the gourd, for which you did not labor; you did not cause it to grow. It came into being in a single night and perished in a single night.

And shall I not care about Nineveh, the great city, in which there are more than one hundred and twenty thousand people who do not know how to distinguish their right hand from their left, and many livestock as well?"
